title: Initiative launched to create Campus Biodiversity Registers across colleges, unversities in India
content:
Nature Conservation Foundation (NCF), a Mysuru-based wildlife research and conservation organisation, and the National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) have launched an initiative to document biodiversity found within college and university campuses across India.
“The CBR project enables educational institutions to document the biodiversity found within their campuses. While the PBRs document the biodiversity of villages, towns, and cities, the focus of the CBRs is on educational institutions, bringing together students, faculty, and other staff to explore nature around them,” a statement from the organisation said.
Conservationists have underlined the importance of college and university campuses as green spaces in India’s expanding urban landscape. Many such institutions harbour a diversity of trees, birds, butterflies, insects, reptiles, mammals, fungi, and countless other organisms.
“These campuses often serve as biodiversity hotspots and refuges within urban landscapes, yet surprisingly little is known about the species they support. Without this knowledge, it is difficult to appreciate, conserve and manage these valuable ecosystems,” the statement read.
Over the course of a year, participating institutions will document biodiversity using the recognised platforms iNaturalist, eBird, and SeasonWatch. “Whether through organised biodiversity surveys, campus walks, or everyday observations, participants will record the plants, animals, fungi, and other life forms they encounter. By the end of the project, each institution will have the information, skills and resources needed to compile its own CBR,” the NCF said.
The biodiversity observations generated through the project are envisaged to contribute to global biodiversity databases while also helping institutions better understand and manage their own campuses. The initiative also aims to foster curiosity about the natural world and encourage a deeper connection between students and the biodiversity that surrounds them.
“This is an opportunity to notice the biodiversity that often goes unnoticed geckos on classroom walls, moths around campus lights, ants around us, and countless other organisms that quietly share our campuses,” NCF’s programme manager Devica Ranade said on Tuesday (July 21, 2026).
NBA Chairperson Virendra Tiwary said India’s youth have a crucial role in fulfilling the nation’s commitment to conservation and sustainable use of biodiversity. “Documenting the biodiversity around them is the first step towards conservation,” he said.

title: Outbound portability of medical scheme benefits Rajasthan’s patients
content:
The outbound portability of the Mukhyamantri Ayushman Aroygya (MAA) Yojana for medical treatment has benefited about 30,000 patients from Rajasthan, who have received free treatment in other States during the last six months.
Over 5,900 claims amounting to ₹17 crore have been submitted by the patients for speciality treatment in States such as Gujarat, Haryana, Maharashtra and Delhi. These treatments included heart stent procedures, heart bypass surgery, cancer surgery, chemotherapy, dialysis and Caesarean deliveries.
State portability was activated earlier this year to give beneficiaries under the MAA Yojana access to 16,000 government and 14,000 private hospitals across the country. Premier institutions such as AIIMS Delhi and Bhopal, PGI Chandigarh, Medanta, KGMU Lucknow and U.N. Mehta Institute of Cardiology, Ahmedabad, were included in the scheme.
Principal Medical and Health Secretary Gayatri Rathore said here on Tuesday (July 21, 2026) that the eligible beneficiaries were availing of cashless treatment worth up to ₹25 lakh at the hospitals empanelled under the Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PMJAY) in various States.
The integration of MAA Yojana with PMJAY has ensured free and cashless treatment for patients beyond the State’s borders. “Every eligible person from Rajasthan can access quality medical treatment even outside the State’s boundaries without any financial burden when the need arises,” Ms. Rathore said.
There are 1.36 crore eligible families under the MAA Yojana in the State. The scheme includes 2,179 treatment packages, covering common ailments as well as serious conditions such as cancer, heart disease, kidney disease and organ transplants.

title: Farmers stopped at Punjab-Haryana border en route to Delhi rally against India-U.S. trade deal
content:
Farmers from Punjab under the banner of ‘Desh Bachao Morcha’ (save the country rally) were on Tuesday (July 21, 2026) stopped by the police at Shambhu-Ambala and Khanauri-Jind, the inter-State boundaries between Haryana and Punjab, as they were heading to Delhi to participate in a rally against the proposed India-U.S. trade deal. The farmers later concluded their protest and announced their return after an assurance of their demands to be taken up with the Centre
layer barricades installed by the Haryana Police at the inter-State boundaries. Ambala police has issued a traffic advisory asking people to take alternative roads as the Shambhu-Ambala road has been closed at the inter-State boundary.
Farmers wanted to peacefully go to Delhi and participate in the rally, but the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)-led Haryana government had stopped them by erecting barricades, Sarwan Singh Pandher, farmer leader and coordinator of the Desh Bachao Morcha, said. “We are ready to talk to the local administration and are in no mood for confrontation,” he said.
Sarwan Singh Pandher, farmer leader and coordinator of the Desh Bachao Morcha, said farmers do not want any confrontation. “Haryana’s Agriculture Minister assured the immediate release of detained farmers and a meeting with Union Agriculture Minister Shivraj Singh Chouhan. Following this, we have concluded our protests at Shambhu and Khanauri,” he said.
“If our concerns continue to be ignored and the agreement is pushed forward, the movement will be intensified,” he added.
Farmers associated with the Desh Bachao Morcha, an umbrella body of farmer unions from different States of the country, had announced they would hold a rally in New Delhi on July 21. The India-U.S. trade deal is not limited merely to the import and export of a few products, the Desh Bachao Morcha said, adding that the deal may instead cover several crucial sectors, including agriculture, dairy, industry, digital trade, services, intellectual property rights, energy, investment, and market access.
Meanwhile, a key trade body has raised concerns surrounding the economic, industrial and public hardships being caused by the repeated closure of the ‘Shambhu Border’ due to the ongoing farmers’ protest.
President of All Industries and Trade Forum, Badish Jindal in a statement said, the repeated closure of Shambhu Border, which is one of the busiest transportation corridors connecting Punjab with Haryana, Delhi and the rest of India, has forced vehicles to travel long alternative routes, resulting in a substantial increase in transportation costs, fuel consumption and transit time.
“Industries are facing delays in receiving raw materials and dispatching finished goods, leading to cancellation of orders, breach of delivery commitments, increased logistics expenses and loss of competitiveness in domestic and international markets. Punjab is already witnessing industrial migration to neighbouring states because of various challenges. The continued disruption of one of the State’s most important transport corridors further weakens investor confidence and creates an impression that industrial and commercial activities in Punjab are becoming increasingly difficult. We urge the government to ask farmer organisations to shift the venue of the protest to a location where it does not completely block one of North India’s most important transport corridors,” he said.
Farmers associated with the Desh Bachao Morcha, an umbrella body of farmer unions from different States of the country, had announced they would hold a rally in New Delhi on July 21.
U.S. trade deal is not limited merely to the import and export of a few products, the Desh Bachao Morcha said, adding that the deal may instead cover several crucial sectors, including agriculture, dairy, industry, digital trade, services, intellectual property rights, energy, investment, and market access.

title: Uttar Pradesh cabinet approves free scooty scheme for college girls
content:
Uttar Pradesh cabinet on Tuesday (July 21, 2026) approved the guidelines for the implementation of the Rani Lakshmibai Scooty Yojana, under which free scooters will be provided to meritorious undergraduate girl students.
reliant and confident, said the State government. Eligible female students will be selected based on merit from the list comprising the top 5% of female graduates.
“The primary objective of this ambitious scheme of the State Government is to reduce the dropout rate among girl students, increase their retention in higher education, ensure their mobility and ease of transportation to higher educational institutions, and improve the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER). Along with this, special emphasis has also been laid on developing a sense of confidence and independence among girl students,”said Yogendra Upadhyay, Higher Education Minister.
Under the scheme, benefits will be extended during the academic session 2025-26 to girl students who are domiciles of Uttar Pradesh and have completed their graduation from State Universities, Private Universities, and Government, Government-Aided and Self-Financed colleges affiliated with State Universities in the State. Eligible female students will be selected based on merit from the list comprising the top 5 percent of female graduates.
According to the Cabinet decision, petrol-powered scooters will be provided under the scheme. The Government has taken this decision keeping in view the better availability of petrol pumps and service centres in remote rural areas of the State. “The scooters will be procured through the Government e-Marketplace [GeM] portal to ensure transparency and quality. The scooter package will include not only the vehicle but also registration charges, comprehensive vehicle insurance, an ISI-marked helmet, 4 litres of petrol, and all necessary accessories, ensuring that the beneficiaries do not have to bear any additional financial burden,” added the government.
U.P. government said that this scheme will increase the participation of daughters in higher education, particularly by making it easier for female students from rural and remote areas to access colleges and universities.

title: Israel says Iran moved thousands of centrifuges to Pickaxe Mountain - Report
content:
Israeli intelligence has assessed that Iran has moved thousands of nuclear centrifuges into a deep underground site in the Natanz area known as Pickaxe Mountain, making them harder to target in aerial strikes, according to a Wall Street Journal report citing US and Israeli officials.
Iran is believed to have made the move last fall, following the 12-day war in June 2025 during which US and Israeli forces bombed Iran's nuclear facilities. The Pickaxe site was not targeted at that time, nor has it been hit since the start of a more intensive air campaign on February 28.
The site, buried over 300 feet under granite, is deeper than enrichment facilities at Natanz and Fordo that were struck in 2025. The Wall Street Journal reported that there has been activity at the site, including truck movements and construction, for approximately 15 months.
Centrifuges source unclear; IAEA denied access
It remains unclear where the centrifuges originated, though they may have been transferred from other Iranian nuclear sites, the report said. Iran has not allowed the International Atomic Energy Agency to access the site and has not provided specific details about its operations.
IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi said in March that Iran had indicated in 2021 that it intended "to have nuclear activity" at Pickaxe, the report said.
US President Donald Trump said last week that "Pickaxe is a possible target for a nice big fat shot right near the front door," adding that the US would "probably give Pickaxe a shot relatively soon."
On Tuesday, Trump told reporters the US would hit the area where Iran's centrifuges are rumoured to be located "pretty soon" and "very heavily," warning Tehran could do nothing to stop it.
Nate Swanson, a former US National Security Council director for Iran during the Biden administration, told the WSJ that Iranian activity at an undeclared site was worrying. "A covert breakout is the worst-case scenario," he said, referring to production of a nuclear weapon. "We don't know what Iran's intentions are, but the fact that we can't verify this site underscores how problematic the status quo is."
Iran maintains its nuclear program is for civilian purposes, but has enriched uranium to just below weapons-grade, a level with no peaceful application.

title: He couldn’t find anyone to fund his movie. Now this Adivasi filmmaker has won a National Film Award
content:
Growing up in a small Adivasi hamlet in Jharkhand’s East Singhbhum district, Ravi Raj Murmu remembers evenings when stories travelled not through books or television but through the voices of elders. Parents and grandparents narrated Santhali folktales, stories of bonga (spirits), forests, rivers and unseen worlds, keeping alive an oral tradition that has shaped generations of Adivasi communities.
Years later, one of those stories has carried Murmu to India’s highest cinematic stage.
Murmu (33), a Santhal filmmaker from Jharkhand, has won the Best Debut Film of a Director at the 72nd National Film Awards for his 12-minute Santhali short film, Angen, whose title loosely translates as “invisible”, “unseen” or “vanished”. While there is no direct English equivalent, Murmu says the word evokes something that exists but gradually slips out of sight.
The film travelled through several festivals, including the Mumbai International Film Festival, before finding national recognition. (screenshot)
The recognition is significant. Angen is the first Santhali-language film to win a National Film Award and only the second fiction film made in an Adivasi language to be recognised at the stage. The first was Edpa Kana (Going Home), a Kurukh-language short film by Oraon filmmaker Niranjan Kujur, which won the Best Audiography award at the 63rd National Film Awards in 2016. Earlier National Award-winning films by Adivasi filmmakers had been made in Hindi.
For Murmu, the award is less about personal recognition than about the stories he has always wanted to tell. “Every story I write comes from Jharkhand,” he said. “They are rooted in Adivasi life. At one point, I realised that if I wanted to make those films honestly, I had to return home.”
After completing his schooling in his village, Murmu studied Mass Communication in Jamshedpur before joining the Film and Television Institute of India (FTII), Pune, where he specialised in editing from 2016 to 2019. He later worked in Mumbai on projects for OTT platforms, including Netflix, Amazon Prime Video, and JioCinema, as well as advertising films.
Beyond its mythical narrative, Angen reflects on the changing relationship between Adivasi-Indigenous people and their connected roots with nature. (screenshot)
But despite working in the mainstream industry, he found himself returning to the stories of his childhood. “I realised our own stories have immense strength,” he said. “For a long time we grew up watching other people’s stories. I wanted Santhali cinema to tell its own story.”
length film that Murmu has been writing for nearly seven years, Angen eventually took the form of a short film after he struggled to secure funding. “A feature would need an investment of around Rs 25-30 crore,” he said. “Since I couldn’t find producers, I decided to make a short film first to show people what the larger story could become.”
The film travelled through several festivals, including the Mumbai International Film Festival, before finding national recognition.
Adapted from a Santhali folktale, Angen follows Sukku, a flute player whose music enchants a goddess living in the spirit world. Drawn into her realm through water, which serves as a portal between the human and spiritual worlds, Sukku later realises he no longer belongs there and begins searching for his village.
“The search never truly ends,” Murmu said, adding that he deliberately leaves the film open-ended because the larger feature is still being developed.
Beyond its mythical narrative, Angen reflects on the changing relationship between Adivasi-Indigenous people and their connected roots with nature. The movie quietly captures how rapid urbanisation reshapes landscapes once associated with Adivasi spiritual life.
Through metaphor, the film shows how, as urbanisation expands, forests disappear, natural spaces shrink, and even those spiritual spaces begin to vanish.
Water in the movie, Murmu said, was chosen consciously. “Science tells us life began with water. That’s why I made water the medium through which one world connects to another,” he said.
The title too carries layered meanings, he said. “There is no literal translation of Angen,” he said. “You can call it invisible, vanished or unseen, but none of those words fully express what it means.”
The idea to show the disappearing world of Adivasis came after a visit home from Pune. Arriving at the Galudih bus stand near his village, Murmu failed to recognise the place after new infrastructure had transformed the landscape. “That made me realise how quickly places change,” he said. “If we don’t document our stories now, many of them may disappear.”
Made on a modest budget with the support of friends and theatre artists, the film stars NSD graduate Jitray Hansda as the older Sukku, Ramchandran Mardi as the younger Sukku and Saloni Soren in the lead female role.
“I worked with the people who believed in the project,” Murmu said. “Without them, this film wouldn’t have been possible.”
Even as Angen earns national recognition, Murmu has already resumed work on his first feature film, with shooting expected to begin soon.
For an Adivasi filmmaker who grew up listening to stories around village homes, the journey from oral tradition to the National Film Awards marks more than a personal milestone. “It is also a reminder that Adivasi stories in their languages, long confined to memory and folklore, are now finding a place on India’s biggest cinematic stage… this is why representation matters,” he said.

title: Have your say on Aravallis: Supreme Court panel opens 21-day window for feedback
content:
4 min readNew DelhiUpdated: Jul 22, 2026 03:31 AM IST
Powered Committee constituted by the Supreme Court to examine issues surrounding the Aravalli Hills and Ranges Tuesday officially sought representations, suggestions, and inputs from a broad spectrum of stakeholders, including the states across which the range spans.
According to a government press release, the panel has invited comments from environmentalists, conservationists, non-profit organisations, mining leaseholders, project proponents, villagers, farmers, mine workers, and local communities whose livelihoods and well-being are intrinsically tied to the region’s biodiversity.
Submissions, supported “to the extent possible” by documentary evidence or verifiable material, must be submitted via email or an official Google Form within 21 days of the notice.
At over a billion years old, the Aravallis are one of the oldest mountain ranges in the world, formed during the Precambrian Era due to the collision of tectonic plates of the earth’s crust. The 700-odd-km range stretches across four states (Gujarat, Rajasthan, Haryana and Delhi) and 37 districts, with 560 km lying in Rajasthan.
Resolving the 100-metre definition row
The Supreme Court established the panel in June under the leadership of Kanchan Devi, Director General of the Indian Council of Forestry Research and Education (ICFRE), to obtain a “fair, impartial, and independent” assessment. The committee’s mandate is to resolve critical ambiguities in an October 2025 report submitted by an Environment Secretary-led panel. The panel is scheduled to submit its comprehensive report to the court by August 31.
Previously, a Supreme Court bench comprising Chief Justice Surya Kant, Justice J K Maheshwari, and Justice Augustine George Masih stayed the top court’s own earlier order accepting a proposed definition of the Aravalli Hills. That definition had categorised the Aravallis as any landform within the region with an elevation of 100 metres or more above the local relief, including its slopes and adjacent land.
The initial acceptance of the 100-metre elevation benchmark sparked widespread public debate after a series of reports by The Indian Express revealed that the criteria would leave over 90 per cent of the vulnerable hill range outside protection, potentially opening vast tracts to  unchecked mining and construction.
Story continues below this ad
“There has been a significant outcry among environmentalists, who have expressed profound concern about the potential for misinterpretation and proper implementation of the newly adopted definition and this Court’s directions. This public dissent and criticism appear to stem from the perceived ambiguity and lack of clarity in certain terms and directives issued by this court,” the Supreme Court noted in its December 29 order, staying the implementation of the ministry report and court’s November 20 judgment.
The panel’s other members are Subhas Ashutosh, former Director General, Forest Survey of India; Rajendra Kumar Sharma, Director (retired), Geological Survey of India; Brij Mohan Singh Rathore, former joint secretary, Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC); and Professor Ashok K Bhatnagar, former professor and head of Botany, Delhi University.
Further, special invitees who will be associated by the chairperson from time to time include Professor Jagdish Krishnaswamy, Dean, School of Environment and Sustainability, Indian Institute of Human Settlements, and Professor (Dr) Laxmikant Sharma, Central University of Rajasthan. In addition, an officer of the rank of Director in the MoEFCC has to be nominated by the Centre as a member secretary.
The committee has been tasked with looking into a definitive enumeration of specific regions that fall within the scope of the 100-metre definition recommended by the ministry panel. It also has to analyse whether ‘sustainable mining’ or ‘regulated mining’ within the newly demarcated Aravalli areas, notwithstanding regulatory oversight, would result in adverse ecological consequences.

title: Microsoft to fund Mistral's European AI expansion in multibillion-dollar deal
content:
Microsoft has agreed to spend billions of dollars on Mistral’s computing infrastructure in Europe under a deal that will also expand distribution of the French AI startup’s technology through the U.S. cloud and software giant, the companies said on Tuesday.
As part of the agreement, Microsoft Azure customers will be able to develop software using Mistral’s data centres in France, giving Microsoft more capacity in Europe and regulated industries an alternative to U.S.-controlled infrastructure.
Mistral, meanwhile, has added its AI models called Medium 3.5 and OCR 4 to Microsoft’s app builder known as Foundry. Microsoft Copilot Studio has brought on Medium 3.5 as well.
Finally, businesses with independent data centers that access Microsoft services via Azure Local will have the option to run Mistral’s “open” models, which give customers license to develop AI as their own.
The deal underscores growing interest in Europe and elsewhere to reduce dependence on U.S. technology so other countries may have greater say in their future society and economy. It may also help Microsoft meet rising demand for open models.
Though the push for “sovereign” AI is now years old, a U.S. decision last month to pause foreign access to two advanced models from San Francisco-based Anthropic has made technology independence a more urgent issue in Europe.
In a joint interview with Reuters, Microsoft President Brad Smith and Mistral CEO Arthur Mensch said the partnership aimed to deliver such sovereignty while allowing access to U.S. software and security features.
“By putting Mistral’s models on Azure Local and on Mistral’s computational capacity, we can combine American and European technology and do it in a way that provides continuous and assured access,” Smith said.
Decoupling Europe from U.S. technology would be a tall order. Nvidia chips powering the global AI boom, also key to Mistral’s data-center buildout, are American-designed. Nvidia, like Microsoft, is a Mistral investor.
Smith said the deal announced on Tuesday did not include any new financial stake in the startup, and Mensch declined to comment on a Bloomberg News report that Mistral was in talks to raise around €3 billion ($3.4 billion) at a €20 billion valuation.
based lab has come to represent one of Europe’s top hopes in AI. So far it has targeted manufacturing, financial services and defense sales and has won business from France’s armed forces. Its valuation remains dwarfed by U.S. peers such as Anthropic.
Still, Mensch said the deal showed how Microsoft and Mistral were “working together on closing the gap on the infrastructure side in Europe.”
Mistral is targeting 1 gigawatt of compute capacity by 2030, and the Microsoft agreement, specifics of which Mensch declined to provide, validates its strategy.
to-market plan, they added.
“This is going to help both of our companies grow our businesses, unquestionably,” Smith said.

title: Anthropic’s $1.5 billion settlement in authors’ class action copyright lawsuit gets approved
content:
A preliminary approval of the settlement was issued in 2025 by Judge William Alsup of the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, and now Judge Araceli Martinez-Olguin has issued the final approval.
The judge ruled last year that Anthropic’s use of the creative works came under fair use. However, the law took issue with the way Anthropic saved millions of pirated books.
Authors and publishers were slated to receive $3,000 per work across an estimated 500,000 works, with the payments being shared based on ownership rights.
While the settlement is a landmark development, it still represents a mixed result for many authors and creators. Several continue to call for stricter laws to protect creative works from being pirated and/or scraped by AI companies to develop their future models. Other creators’ lawsuits against Anthropic over related issues are still pending.
related use of their works, others want to see violators being more strictly penalised and fined around the world.
Multiple similar lawsuits against AI companies such as Google, Meta, and OpenAI are also ongoing. As Anthropic’s case ended in a settlement in this instance, it remains to be seen how other U.S. judges will interpret book piracy allegations that authors have levelled against AI companies.

title: "The pain is immense," says Messi of World Cup defeat
content:
Lionel Messi said on Monday (July 20, 2026) that after Argentina's World Cup final loss, "the pain is immense and it's going to take a long time for this wound to heal."
"I'd also like to congratulate Spain on winning the tournament," added the 39-year-old, who broke down in tears after the defeat.
In the knockout rounds, Argentina came from behind to beat Egypt and England and had to go to extra time to beat Cape Verde and Switzerland.
"With the matches we turned around by giving our all - matches that will remain in our memories forever - and with the support of an entire country, which, together with the hard work and effort of this squad, led us to once again be among the best in the world," the Inter Miami forward wrote in the post.
Having scored eight goals during the tournament, Messi, an eight-time Ballon d'Or winner as best player in the world, failed to make an impact in the final, much like his team, which was unable to pose a threat to a better-organised Spanish side.
At the World Cup, he has now lost two finals, in 2014 and 2026, and won one, in 2022, against France.
Argentinian media reported on Monday (July 20) that Messi was not expected to make the trip back to Buenos Aires with most of the rest of the squad for what promises to be a subdued homecoming.

title: FIFA investigates Argentina players confronting Spain''s on-field World Cup celebrations
content:
FIFA opened an investigation Monday (July 21, 2026) after Spain's celebrations on beating Argentina in the World Cup final took an ugly turn when players briefly clashed on the field.
Video replays showed several players involved in a scuffle, including Argentina midfielder Leandro Paredes. He appeared to shove Spain's Eric Garcia by the throat. When Spain substitute Gavi jumped in to intervene, Paredes pushed him to the ground.
He appeared to shove Spain's Eric Garcia by the throat. When Spain substitute Gavi jumped in to intervene, Paredes pushed him to the ground.
Spain captain Rodri also seemed to be hit in the stomach by Argentina defender Nahuel Molina as he ran past to join teammates celebrating the title win.
Molina later crowded Rodri, who was the target of a verbal confrontation by his former Manchester City teammate Nicolas Otamendi.
Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni and other players rushed in to deescalate the situation.
FIFA said in a statement that its “disciplinary committee has appointed a Disciplinary and Ethics Prosecutor to investigate potential breaches of the [FIFA disciplinary code] about the post-match incidents.” The brief statement gave no timetable for an investigation and did not name Paredes, who was not shown a red card after the game yet could face a suspension.
Spain needed extra time to win Sunday's final 1-0 after Argentina was reduced to 10 men. Enzo Fernandez was sent off with a second yellow card in stoppage time at the end of 90 minutes.
The Argentina players stood facing their fans, with their backs to the podium, when Spain's squad lifted the trophy. It was an unusual show of disrespect to the new champions, who had given their opponents a guard of honour to collect their medals.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026 | Spain refines its style, rekindles winning spree
content:
two Copa Americas on either side of the 2022 FIFA World Cup. It had not lost a knockout duel since the 2019 Copa America. It had made fighting back from the brink look like clockwork. It had Lionel Messi, the greatest player of this generation and arguably of all time, in its midst.
up in the previous World Cup, and roared with unbridled joy in the edition prior to that. It had the most dazzling array of attacking players in Kylian Mbappe, Michael Olise and Ousmane Dembele.
Yet, when they met Spain at the business end of the just-concluded quadrennial spectacle in the United States, Canada and Mexico, neither seemed to turn up. Or more accurately, they were not allowed to turn up.
flawless campaign — remarkably, the goalless draw against Cape Verde in their opener was the only stutter in the entire tournament — with a 1-0 win over Argentina in the final to lift their second World Cup.
Much like the incandescent march to the title by an elite group of players in 2010, many of whom were in the stands at the New York New Jersey Stadium on Sunday afternoon to watch their countrymen thoroughly outclass Messi’s troops, the Iberian nation’s latest conquest on the grand stage is not merely a victory on the field of play. It is also a renewed vindication of an idea and identity that is rooted in a deep affiliation for keeping the ball.
up dates back to Luis Aragones’ time as head coach in the mid-2000s. Having overseen Spain’s round-of-16 exit to France at the 2006 World Cup in Germany, the bespectacled tactician paved the way for a course-correction by trading La Furia Roja — a more direct, physical approach — for a possession-based game.
That he had midfielders of the ilk of Xavi and Andres Iniesta, who had been schooled in this philosophy at La Masia — Barcelona’s renowned youth academy — and were beginning to come into their own, facilitated a smooth-sailing transition. As intended, Spain found long-awaited success at the 2008 European Championship.
For the idea to become the envy of the footballing world, the entry of Pep Guardiola as Barcelona manager in the summer of 2008 was even more significant. With Messi, Xavi, Iniesta and Sergio Busquets among his core contingent, Guardiola elevated that style, modelled on intricate passing, playing out from the back and retaining possession, to another degree. Tiki-taka would become an integral part of the sport’s lexicon and embody the beautiful game in all its glory.
At the international level, Vicente del Bosque, who took over from Aragones as Spain coach after Euro 2008, knew better than to veer away from these tried-and-tested tactics. More success inevitably came Spain’s way at the 2010 World Cup and Euro 2012.
Ever since, the importance of keeping the ball has been drilled into Spain’s footballers across all levels from a very early age. Pressing opponents high up the pitch as soon as they lose possession is another ingredient fundamental to this playbook. In a nutshell, no team retains possession better than Spain, and nobody recovers the ball as quickly. That is a surefire way of winning football matches, isn’t it?
Even so, Spain stagnated and fell into a rut where it didn’t win a lot of World Cup games between 2012 and 2024. In the carnival in Brazil in 2014, the defending champion was jolted by a shock group-stage exit.
In 2018 and 2022, it couldn’t advance past the round of 16. In Qatar four years ago, Spain’s manner of defeat to Morocco was particularly jarring, for its ouster via a penalty shootout after a drab 120 minutes was marked by a distinct absence of penetration in the final third. Pass after pass was played without an end product. Spain’s obsession with possession, perhaps rightly, was labelled by its critics as dour and devoid of imagination.
Few would have anticipated, then, that Luis de la Fuente would be the man to engineer a turnaround. Appointed in December 2022 in the immediate aftermath of a disastrous World Cup that Luis Enrique paid the price for, de la Fuente, 61 then, was barely known beyond his country’s borders.
Much to the credit of the federation, what he did have in spades was an intimate understanding of the players he was going to work with. He had played a pivotal part in the development of many of them in his capacity as coach of Spain’s junior teams, winning the U-19 European Championship in 2015 and the U-21 equivalent in 2019. He knew the buttons to press to get the best out of these players.
Instead of throwing the baby out with the bathwater, de la Fuente shrewdly stuck to the principal identity of Spanish football in the 21st century and embellished it with his own beliefs. Instead of the slow, sometimes monotonous build-up that involved a sequence of short passes in search for the perfect goal, de la Fuente would set up his team to be more fluid in its use of a large share of possession. The former full-back employed greater attacking width, and desired the ball circulating more quickly through the middle of the park.
Back to being the best
The results have been resounding. Having won every game on the way to claiming a record fourth European title in 2024, the Spanish juggernaut stitched together an unbeaten run at this World Cup too.
In Sunday’s final, Messi and Co. were a distant second. As is its wont, Spain had 60 percent of possession, and 20 attempts in comparison to Argentina’s two. The Albiceleste’s first shot came during the second half of extra time. Not one was on target.
Such supremacy wouldn’t have been possible without the calming, all-encompassing influence of Rodri. Blessed with the passing range of a playmaker and the physicality of a centre-back, the 6’3” defensive midfielder combines the dual responsibility of orchestrating Spain’s attacks and suppressing the rival’s threats with aplomb. As per Opta, the 30-year-old delivered 122 line-breaking passes at this World Cup, the most in a single edition since compatriot Xabi Alonso’s 126 in 2010.
To be presented the Golden Ball at the end of Sunday’s final — awarded to the best player of the tournament — was a fitting recognition of his contribution over the past month. If he has a robust beginning to the club season with Manchester City, a second Ballon d’Or could be another feather in an ornate cap by the end of the year.
against Belgium in the quarterfinals — was also down to goalkeeper Unai Simon and a well-drilled defensive unit. While Simon wasn’t really required to make desperate saves worthy of making the highlight packages, his inclination to step up as a sweeper, a la Germany’s Manuel Neuer from recent memory, enabled Spain’s defence to hold a high line. There were instances in both the semifinal and final when the French and Argentine forwards successfully beat the offside trap and ran behind the defence, but Simon was ever so swift to charge out of the box and clear the danger.
In keeping a clean sheet in seven out of eight matches, the precocious Pau Cubarsi also played a sparkling part. All of 19, the centre-back’s positional awareness and reading of the game were well beyond his tender age. It meant that he was one of only two outfield players, the other being left-back Marc Cucurella, to play every minute of the competition for Spain.
knit defensive unit working in tandem, Spain’s forwards were never encumbered with having to chase a game. Not once, in fact, did the European giant trail in its quest for the holy grail, a first in 23 editions cutting across 96 years. The attack wasn’t free-flowing to start with, struggling to get past the gutsy Vozinha in the Cape Verde goal despite enjoying a plethora of possession. It may have invited some doubts about Spain retreating to a turgid style.
again Lamine Yamal and Dani Olmo into the mix from the get-go in the second game against Saudi Arabia, the dynamism in Spain’s attacking play returned and it romped to a 4-0 win. While Olmo’s foremost strength is his ability to receive the ball on the half-turn and dribble his way through congested spaces, Yamal’s trickery on the right wing is a sight for sore eyes.
The wunderkind sparkled in spurts without quite seizing the spotlight as a teenaged Pele did in 1958 or Mbappe in 2018. A torn left hamstring at the back end of a hectic season with Barcelona may have been a factor, but it is instructive of the high regard he is held in that opponents yet felt the need to often double up against the 19-year-old.
Yamal’s scope for growing into a great of the game applies to many of the other members too. Sample the case of Nico Williams. The 24-year-old has pace to burn on the left wing, but circumstances curtailed his impact at this event. One of the breakthrough stars of the Euros two summers ago, Williams came into this year’s marquee competition with a hamstring injury.
To compound matters, he suffered a right adductor muscle injury in the final group encounter against Uruguay. Still, he came off the bench and provided the assist for Ferran Torres’ 106th-minute winner in the summit clash.
The conclusion of a World Cup, regardless of the outcome, usually marks the end of a cycle. With so many in this champion outfit yet to hit their prime, however, this feels like just the start of a very special era. After winning the World Cup in 2010, the men in bright red had to wait 16 years for a second star to be emblazoned above their crest. The third is likely to come far sooner.

title: Possession with purpose unlocks Spain’s World Cup puzzle
content:
The oldest footballing lesson in Spain has always been deceptively simple. If you have the ball, the opponent cannot score.
For years, however, that philosophy became both its greatest gift and its greatest burden. Spain monopolised possession, accumulated passes and controlled territory, but too often it left tournaments wondering whether beautiful football alone could still win the biggest prizes. The echoes of tiki-taka lingered long after the golden generation had faded, sometimes becoming an identity that imprisoned rather than liberated.
At this World Cup, Spain did not abandon its football. It rediscovered its purpose.
Luis de la Fuente’s world champions finished the tournament with the best defence, conceding only one goal in eight matches. They dominated possession as La Roja always have, but possession was no longer the destination. It became the platform from which every other part of the game flourished. They pressed ferociously when they lost the ball, attacked vertically when spaces opened and defended with a discipline that suffocated some of the world’s finest forwards.
Against Belgium in the quarterfinals, Spain patiently waited until Mikel Merino struck the decisive late winner. Against Portugal, it accepted a tactical battle and found the goal required to make it past the Iberian neighbours. In the final, it denied Lionel Messi the fairytale ending that seemed almost scripted.
Every challenge demanded something different and Spain always had an answer.
That adaptability perhaps best explains why this team succeeded where previous Spanish sides had fallen. The Spain of Vicente del Bosque often appeared convinced that enough possession would eventually solve every problem. De la Fuente’s Spain understood that football offers many different questions.
Sometimes the answer was Lamine Yamal. Still only a teenager, Yamal entered the tournament carrying expectations that would have burdened far older footballers. His acceleration, disguised passing and fearlessness repeatedly tilted matches before opponents had organised themselves. Yet unlike so many young stars, he was never asked to carry Spain alone. Around him functioned a collective that was entirely free of hierarchy.
Rodri remained the team’s metronome, rarely hurried, forever choosing the correct pass before pressure arrived. He has now won the Player of the Tournament both in the Euro and the World Cup. Fabian Ruiz produced perhaps the finest tournament of his career, knitting midfield together while contributing goals and relentless defensive work. Nico Williams stretched defences from the opposite flank whenever he played, while Dani Olmo drifted between lines, creating overloads wherever Spain needed them. Mikel Oyarzabal provided the goals to soothe down the nerves after the initial draw with Cape Verde.
backs pushed high enough to create width without exposing the defence and goalkeeper Unai Simon was called upon surprisingly infrequently, a reflection of how effectively those in front of him protected the penalty boxes. Whenever Spain lost possession, the counter-press arrived almost instinctively, often recovering the ball before opponents could string together three passes.
Every attacker defended. Every defender initiated attacks. Possession, pressing and positional discipline became parts of the same movement rather than separate ideas.
There was also a maturity that distinguished this side from many previous champions.
Spain rarely chased matches emotionally. It never seemed rushed by the occasion, whether facing Kylian Mbappe in Dallas, Belgium in Los Angeles or Lionel Messi in the MetLife Stadium. Its football carried the serenity of a team that knew exactly who it was.
For much of the past decade, international football has been shaped by transition, athleticism and chaos. Spain reminded everyone that control still matters. But it also demonstrated that control no longer means simply completing 800 passes.
It means controlling space, tempo, emotions and ultimately the result.
In doing so, de la Fuente’s players achieved something larger than lifting the World Cup. They completed Spanish football’s evolution.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026: A grand spectacle despite its share of controversies
content:
For six weeks, the country that had spent decades calling football soccer could talk about little else.
The game poured into the United States through its airports and railway stations, occupied its downtowns and suburbs, and turned stadium parking lots into temporary republics of colour, song and shared obsession. Argentinians carried their mate through hotel lobbies. Mexicans arrived with sombreros and booming brass bands. Brazilians brought drums, Colombians danced, the English colonised pubs and supporters from countries separated by oceans discovered one another on trains, concourses and pavements.
In New York, Philadelphia, Boston, Atlanta, Miami, Dallas, Los Angeles and the other host cities, football was no longer an immigrant game.
For one extraordinary summer, it had taken over the American psyche.
enormous arenas, endless commercial possibilities, a culture fluent in spectacle and a population drawn from nearly every footballing nation on earth. The World Cup brought all those elements together on a scale the tournament had never previously attempted.
eight nations, 1,039 players and 104 matches featured in the tournament co-hosted by the USA, Mexico and Canada. A record 6,810,966 spectators attended, more than the combined totals of the previous two World Cups in Russia and Qatar. The 16 venues operated at 99.7% capacity, with an average attendance of 65,490.
The figures described the tournament’s size. They could not fully capture its presence.
There were supporters who crossed continents and families who crossed generations. Fathers who had discovered Argentina through Diego Maradona returned with sons who had fallen in love with Lionel Messi. Children wore the shirts of countries they had never visited. Diaspora communities that usually experienced international football through television suddenly found their teams appearing a short drive away.
A Brazilian home match could emerge in New Jersey. Argentina could turn Atlanta or Dallas into Buenos Aires for an evening. The Haitians could revel in defeat, while the African diaspora celebrated the march of nine of the continent’s nations into the knockout rounds.
America did not simply host the world. Much of the world was already living within it.
That was why the atmosphere felt different from the carefully imported FIFA spectacle. The passion did not have to be manufactured. It existed in Queens and New Jersey, in South Florida’s Latin American communities, among West Africans in the Washington corridor and South Asians who followed Argentina from one end of the country to the other.
They filled the spaces between the matches. They turned hotel breakfasts into fan gatherings, railway platforms into debates and city streets into improvised parades. The World Cup became visible even to Americans who had no ticket and little previous relationship with the sport.
And the football rewarded them.
The expanded tournament offered concerns about excess, but it also allowed unfamiliar stories to develop alongside the established powers. Cape Verde supplied one of its most romantic journeys. Norway and Erling Haaland eliminated Brazil. Egypt reached the pre-quarterfinals and pushed Argentina to the edge. By the semifinals, the old hierarchy had returned through Spain, Argentina, France and England, but the road towards it had been crowded with resistance.
The leading players who arrived with reputations largely lived up to them. Messi extended his World Cup appearance record to 34 matches and, at 39 years and 25 days, became the oldest outfield player to feature in a final. Kylian Mbappe, Haaland, Harry Kane and Jude Bellingham contributed goals and moments worthy of the stage. Rodri controlled the tournament’s decisive matches and was rightly named its finest player, not overshadowed by the glamorous goal-scorers who have a penchant of hogging all the attention.
Spain’s teenagers offered a glimpse of what might come next. Lamine Yamal and Pau Cubarsí became only the fourth and fifth teenagers to appear in a men’s World Cup final, joining Pele, Giuseppe Bergomi and Mbappe in the exclusive group to have done so and finished as champions. Their eight appearances were also the most played at a World Cup before turning 20.
The tournament became a meeting point between football’s past and future. Messi pursued one last title while Yamal began building a legacy of his own.
There was entertainment almost everywhere. The 308 goals surpassed the previous tournament record of 172, while the average of 2.96 per game was the highest since the 1970 World Cup in Mexico. Long-range shooting returned as a significant weapon, while late goals repeatedly transformed knockout matches.
Arsene Wenger, FIFA’s chief of global football development, believed the tournament would be remembered for its physical intensity, increased competitive balance and the performances of its biggest stars. Gianni Infantino went considerably further, declaring that the World Cup had not merely broken records but “smashed” and “destroyed” them.
FIFA rarely misses an opportunity to proclaim its own success.
But this was not a flawless tournament.
There were moments when football’s American takeover began to resemble football being taken over by America.
minute hydration breaks, even in air-conditioned or closed-roof stadiums, creating pauses conveniently suited to television advertising. The final received a Super Bowl-style extended half-time show. Commercial messaging seemed to occupy almost every unclaimed second, leaving traditionalists wondering whether FIFA had discovered new ways to monetise the game’s natural rhythm.
Ticket prices placed many matches beyond the reach of ordinary supporters, even if the stands ultimately remained full. Travel between distant host cities was expensive and exhausting. The scale that made the tournament historic also made it cumbersome.
There were more serious concerns around refereeing and VAR. Portugal’s victory over Croatia became an almost continuous video review, with four goals disallowed in a single match. Egypt’s dramatic defeat to Argentina produced accusations of inconsistency after a goal was ruled out, appeals for a late penalty involving Mohamed Salah were rejected and Argentina scored its winner soon afterwards. The Egyptian Football Association formally questioned the fairness and transparency of the officiating.
Argentina’s quarterfinal against Switzerland brought further arguments over a red-card decision, while a tournament promoted as the most technologically advanced in history often demonstrated that more cameras did not necessarily produce greater agreement.
Nothing damaged that trust more than the case of Folarin Balogun. The US striker was sent off following a VAR review, ordinarily carrying an automatic one-match suspension. But after President Donald Trump telephoned Infantino and publicly intervened, FIFA lifted the ban, allowing Balogun to face Belgium. FIFA argued that its disciplinary committee had independently determined that the offence did not merit suspension, but the optics were devastating.
Belgium coach Rudi Garcia called it a decision that would hurt both the World Cup and the US. UEFA officials viewed it as a breach of established protocol, while the episode created the impression that football’s laws could be renegotiated through access to political power.
It was an uncomfortable reminder that FIFA’s closeness to the American President had become one of the tournament’s persistent subplots.
That relationship reached its awkward conclusion during the final presentation.
After Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 in New Jersey, Trump joined Infantino to present the medals and trophy. He was loudly booed by sections of the crowd but remained on the podium after Rodri had received the World Cup, standing prominently among the Spanish players as they started their celebrations.
a President accustomed to occupying every available camera angle standing beside a team whose greatest moment should have belonged entirely to them.
For all FIFA’s excess, the political intrusion, the refereeing arguments and the relentless commercialisation, the tournament demonstrated something long suspected but never previously tested on such a scale.
The US is ready for football.
Not merely for a version of it packaged as occasional entertainment, stripped of its culture and sold between other American sports. It was ready for the full, unruly game: the tribalism and tension, the tactical arguments, the national longing, the heartbreak of elimination and the songs that continued long after the players had left.
The country did not suddenly abandon baseball, basketball or American football. Nor did everyone stop calling the game soccer. But for six weeks, the distinction had hardly mattered.
Americans learned the names. They debated VAR. They complained about referees. They arranged work and travel around kick-off times. They discovered that a scoreless match could be gripping despite their own sports regularly producing double-triple digit scores.
The World Cup came seeking a host. It left with a newfound audience.

title: Qamar fondly recalls his historic CWG gold medal
content:
As 14 Indian boxers get ready to compete in the Glasgow Commonwealth Games this week, Mohammed Ali Qamar — the first pugilist from the country to win a gold medal in the quadrennial event at Manchester 24 years ago — looks back at his historic feat with pride.
Qamar was the surprise package of the 2002 edition. “Our team had big names, including 1998 Asian Games gold medallist (the late) Dingko Singh and Jitender Kumar. When Dingko lost early, and with limited means of communication to stay in touch, my family took it for granted that I must have lost as well. Later, they were delighted,” Qamar told The Hindu.
“Nowadays our boxers are getting so much support and even camping in Ireland before the CWG. But we just trained in Patiala. Our foreign coach, Peter Stoyanov of Bulgaria, made us train at 11.30 at night so that we got adjusted to the UK time zone before landing in Manchester five-six days prior to the Games. At the camp, there was just one physio for boxing, athletics and weightlifting, and one or two masseurs.”
The 46-year-old from Kolkata fondly remembered his 48kg final bout, in which he rallied to beat the host nation’s Darren Langley 27-25. “There used to be running scores in those days, but they weren’t shown consistently. After the penultimate round, a Malaysian coach watched the scores on TV and informed our fellow boxers in the stands that I was trailing by five points. They relayed it to our ringside coaches. I went all-out and won the third round.
“In the final, I used a trick I had learnt from the Cubans. I lifted my hand every time I landed a punch in order to convince the judges! Winning the gold was a special moment.”
An Arjuna and Dronacharya awardee, Qamar, a former head coach of the Indian women’s team, expects good things from Glasgow. “Most of our women boxers, including Lovlina Borgohain and Jaismine Lamboria, are experienced. They should do well. Some of our men, such as Sachin Siwach and Narender Berwal, also have proven track records,” said Qamar.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026 | Messi leaves the World Cup as its most enduring presence
content:
For 120 minutes, Spain did what no other team at this World Cup had managed.
It found a way to keep Lionel Messi quiet.
The plan was simple in theory but brutally difficult in practice. Every time the 39-year-old drifted towards the right flank, another red shirt arrived. His every passing lane closed and every familiar slalom towards his left foot was met by bodies rather than space.
“The first idea was to keep Messi at bay,” Spain coach Luis de la Fuente admitted afterwards. He had learned his lesson when a 16-year-old Messi had terrorised his Sevilla team in an under-19 match in 2004.
But here, rather than man-marking the Argentine talisman, the Spanish coach looked at cutting the supply and isolating Messi. The Argentine captain managed only 15 touches in the first half and 54 across the entire final. There was no trademark curling finish, no defence-splitting pass, no decisive intervention.
The overwhelming majority of the 80,663 spectators had come hoping to witness another coronation. They wanted to see the man who had already given them one of football’s greatest endings add one final chapter by lifting a second World Cup.
Instead, the faithful watched him leave in tears.
It was here in MetLife in 2016, when Messi had missed in the Copa America final shootout against Chile and announced his international retirement before making a comeback 47 days later to complete his redemption arc.
Ten years later, he returned to the same stadium seeking one final crown, only for it to deny him again.
As Spain celebrated at the centre circle, Messi and his teammates walked towards the Argentina supporters gathered behind the southern goal. The applause that greeted them was as loud as anything heard all evening. Spain’s players, too, understood the moment.
Several, including Lamine Yamal, crossed the pitch to embrace Messi after the final whistle. Later, as Argentina climbed the podium to collect its runner-up medals, the new world champion formed a guard of honour. A few patted him on the back, Messi tried to smile, but the disappointment was hard to contain.
One match, however, cannot rewrite a career.
Messi leaves the World Cup as its most enduring presence with 34 appearances, 23 victories, 21 goals, 27 matches as captain, seven goals from outside the penalty area, 10 knockout-stage assists and, above all, the man who finally completed football’s greatest story by lifting the trophy in 2022. He remains the only player to score in every knockout round of a World Cup-winning campaign.
Spain may have denied him one final triumph, but no one can take away the imprint he has left on this tournament.
For generations to come, the World Cup’s story will be retold through many champions, but few names will echo as deeply as that of Lionel Messi’s.

title: India name 20-member squad for Hockey World Cup, Harmanpreet Singh to lead
content:
India on Tuesday (July 21, 2026) named a 20-member squad for next month's FIH Men's Hockey World Cup, with four players from the side who featured in the European leg of the Pro League missing out owing to the tournament's squad-size limit.
Defender Amandeep Lakra, midfielders Raj Kumar Pal and Rabichandra Singh Moirangthem, and forward Selvam Karthi were the four players omitted from the 24-member Pro League squad.
India endured a disappointing FIH Pro League campaign last month, winning four, drawing five and losing seven matches to finish eighth among nine teams, just above Pakistan.
The side will now look to improve on their recent World Cup record.
India finished ninth in the previous edition in 2023 and have not won any medal since claiming their lone title in Kuala Lumpur in 1975. They had earlier secured bronze in Barcelona in 1971 and silver in Amsterdam in 1973.
flicker and defender Harmanpreet Singh, who led India to Olympic bronze medal at the 2024 Paris Games, will captain the side.
Veteran midfielder Manpreet Singh, India's most-capped player, adds experience to a squad blending established stars with promising youngsters.
"This is a well-balanced squad — the right mix of tournament experience and in-form youth who've earned their spot through performance, not reputation,” chief coach Craig Fulton said.
The goalkeeping department comprises Mohith HS and Suraj Karkera, while the defensive unit features Jarmanpreet Singh, captain Harmanpreet, Amit Rohidas, Jugraj Singh, Sanjay, Sumit and Yashdeep Siwach.
The midfield will be led by Manpreet, Hardik Singh and Vivek Sagar Prasad, alongside Nilakanta Sharma and youngsters Rajinder Singh and Aditya Arjun Lalage.
The forward line consists of Mandeep Singh, Dilpreet Singh, Abhishek, Sukhjeet Singh and Shilanand Lakra.
India have been drawn in Pool D alongside England, Pakistan and Wales, with all their group-stage matches scheduled to be played in Amstelveen, the Netherlands.
“Press, counter, perform isn't just our tactical identity — it's how this group trains and thinks together every single day. We are not getting ahead of ourselves — one game at a time, all in, is the mentality we're taking into every match,” Fulton added.
India will open their campaign against Wales on August 15 before facing England on August 17 and arch-rivals Pakistan on August 19 in what is expected to be the marquee clash of the group stage.
“Fifty years since 1975 (World Cup win), this group has a chance to write its own chapter in Indian hockey history, and we are really excited about it," Fulton concluded.

title: TOI's XI: The stars who lit up the FIFA World Cup 2026
content:
The North American odyssey is over.
Here are the stars who put their best foot forward.
Pedro Porro (ESP, Defender) One of the tournament’s most attack-minded full-backs, Porro has balanced defensive discipline with constant overlapping runs. His accurate deliveries and relentless energy have given Spain width, while his recovery pace has helped neutralise dangerous wingers in the knockout games.
Cristian Romero (ARG, Defender) Romero has been Argentina’s defensive warrior, combining aggression with intelligent positioning. The centre-back has thrived in highpressure knockout matches, making crucial interceptions and blocks while providing the steel behind Lionel Scaloni’s side as they played another World Cup final.
Unai Simon (ESP, Goalkeeper) Spain’s last line of defence has been calmness personified throughout the tournament. Simón has produced crucial saves in the knockout rounds while commanding a backline that has conceded just once. His composure, positioning and distribution have been instrumental in Spain’s march to the title.
Pau Cubarsi (ESP, Defender) Still only a teenager, Cubarsi has played with remarkable maturity on the biggest stage.
onone situations, the Barcelona defender has marshalled Spain’s defence with authority, helping champions La Roja boast the tournament’s stingiest backline.
Marc Cucurella (ESP, Defender) Has enjoyed outstanding World Cup, shutting down elite attackers while contributing in possession. Tireless up and down the left flank, he has combined defensive resilience with attacking support, earning widespread praise as one of Spain’s most consistent performers.
Michael Olise (FRA, Attacking midfielder) Olise has emerged as France’s chief creator, dazzling with his dribbling, vision and precise final ball. His creativity has unlocked stubborn defences repeatedly, making him one of the tournament’s standout playmakers despite Les Bleus missing out on another World Cup final.
Rodri (ESP, Midfielder) The heartbeat of Spain’s midfield, Rodri has dictated the tempo with supreme composure. His interceptions, passing range and tactical intelligence have allowed Spain to dominate possession throughout. He has once again showed why he remains among football’s finest holding midfielders. He won the Golden Ball.
Jude Bellingham (ENG, Attacking midfielder) England’s driving force in midfield, Bellingham has mixed elegance with relentless intensity. He has chipped in with vital goals, created chances and controlled games with maturity beyond his years, standing out despite England falling short of the World Cup final.
Lionel Messi (ARG, Forward) At 39, Messi continues to redefine greatness. The Argentine captain has led from the front with decisive goals, assists and moments of brilliance, inspiring his side into a second successive World Cup final. His vision, composure and leadership have once again made him the game’s gold standard.
Erling Haaland (NOR, Striker) Haaland announced Norway’s arrival among world football’s elite with a sensational tournament. His physical dominance, clinical finishing and relentless movement powered Norway into the quarterfinals. He staked his claim as the game’s leading marksman and the best in the No. 9 position.
Kylian Mbappe (FRA, Forward) France’s biggest attacking threat once again, Mbappe has combined explosive pace with ruthless finishing till the semifinal. Whether scoring or creating, he has tormented defenders in every round and claimed the Golden Boot despite France’s semifinal exit.
Coach Luis de la Fuente SPAIN Masterminded Spain’s triumphant campaign with a fluid possession game, relentless highpressing and collective discipline. Having nurtured many players through Spain’s youth ranks, he built a cohesive unit that outplayed every opponent, proving once again that the strength of the team outweighed individual brilliance.

title: Sreeshankar eyes golden leap at CWG 2026: 'Hopefully change the colour of medal'
content:
Just over two years ago,
Murali Sreeshankar feared he might never compete again. A ruptured patellar tendon during training in April 2024 forced the Indian long jumper to miss his biggest dream - the Paris Olympics - despite having already secured qualification.
Doctors, coaches and many around him believed his career could be over. But he refused to give up.
Today, the 27-year-old is back stronger than ever.
After winning silver at the 2022 Commonwealth Games with 8.08m, Sreeshankar now has only one goal in mind - to turn silver into gold.
"Because last time I won silver with the same jump, 8.08. I am in good touch, more confident. This time the chances are looking really good too. Hopefully change the color of the medal," Sreeshankar told reporters during a virtual media interaction.
I am in the best condition
The 2025 season was all about rebuilding for Sreeshankar, both mentally and physically.
After surgery, he shifted his focus to the Commonwealth Games, the Asian Games and, eventually, the LA Olympics.
"My main goal in 2025 was to be at the World Championship so that I set the tone for the 2026 season, which is exactly happening right now. Now in 2026 we have Commonwealth Games in 10 days and in two-and-a-half months we have the Asian Games as well. It's about tweaking your preparation to make sure that you peak at the right time," he said.
"The global standards have become higher, but the biggest confidence for me is that physically I am in the best condition ever after surgery. During rehabilitation I worked on a lot of factors that really improved my physical condition and qualities. I'm really optimistic and looking forward to the events, especially the Commonwealth Games where the competition will be very high. I'm excited to go to Glasgow and give a good competition," he added.
Preparing in Poland for the Glasgow challenge
Sreeshankar has been currently training in Spala, Poland, where the conditions closely resemble what he expects in Glasgow. The Indian star believes adapting to the Mondo track surface and the cooler weather could make a major difference.
"Spala is absolutely the best place to train right now, not just because of the weather but also because of the track. The track here is Mondo, which is the same surface we will have in Glasgow," he said.
"When I came here to Spala, the first week was difficult because the feedback you get from a Mondo track is completely different from a normal track. We've been training outdoors during evening sessions when it's colder so my body gets adjusted," the long jumper added.
Recovery now matters more than workload
After the serious knee injury, Sreeshankar says recovery has become just as important as training.
"Workload-wise, recovery is the most important thing now. More than working hard and going all out every single day, recovery has become the key part of my training. I basically train five days a week. One day is active recovery and one day is complete rest because I can't train the way I trained five years ago because of my knee issue."
He added that years of elite competition have also taken their toll.
After the Commonwealth Games, scheduled to be held from July 23 to August 2, there will be little time to shift focus to the Asian Games. The 20th Asian Games will be held in Aichi and Nagoya from September 19 to October 4.
With both the Commonwealth Games and Asian Games coming within months, Sreeshankar knows the challenge lies in timing his peak.
"The challenge is to peak at two different stages, the Commonwealth Games and the Asian Games. You have to respect your body and give it proper recovery so that it is ready for the next training session. That's what professional sport is all about, managing recovery and training schedules," he said.
No target distance, only trust in the process
Over the years, Sreeshankar has taken his performances to another level. His personal best of 8.41m, set at the Federation Cup in Bhubaneswar in June 2023, remains the national record.
Despite jumping 8.38m this season, Sreeshankar believes there is much more to come.
"I haven't kept a ceiling for my jump because I feel this season I have the potential to produce a very big jump. I haven't fixed any particular mark like a national record or any specific distance. I'm just trusting the process and working on every parameter that is in my control. I'm optimistic that the big jump will come in a major championship," he said.
"I was very happy because it was my best jump after surgery, but there is still a much bigger jump waiting. Once all the different parameters align properly, I'll get that big jump. I'm eagerly waiting for that day," Sreeshankar said.

title: 'World Cup lost its credibility': Blatter says politics overshadowed football
content:
Former FIFA president Sepp Blatter has launched a scathing attack on football's governing body in the aftermath of the 2026
FIFA World Cup, claiming the tournament "lost its credibility" and urging fans, players and national associations to reclaim the sport from political influence.
Blatter's strongly worded message came just two days after Spain defeated defending champions Argentina 1-0 after extra time to lift their second World Cup title, bringing the first-ever 48-team World Cup to a close.
While acknowledging Spain as deserving champions, the 90-year-old Swiss did not hold back in his assessment of the tournament's wider legacy.
Taking to X, Blatter wrote: "The longest World Cup has come to an end – with the right champions. But on the way to the final whistle, the tournament had lost its credibility. Politics must not be allowed to overshadow the game."
He concluded with an even stronger appeal directed at football's stakeholders.
"Now it is up to the fans, players and national associations to reclaim football and bring it back to its roots under new leadership," he added.
The post also tagged FIFA, the World Cup, the controversial FIFA halftime show, Donald Trump and current FIFA president Gianni Infantino, underlining who Blatter believes should be at the centre of the debate.
Politics and controversies dominate tournament discussion
Blatter has been one of the most vocal critics of FIFA's direction under Infantino, and his latest remarks reflect growing scrutiny over the intersection of football and politics during the expanded tournament.
The 2026 edition, jointly hosted by the United States, Canada and Mexico, was the biggest in World Cup history, featuring 48 teams, 104 matches across 16 venues over more than a month.
Although the football itself received widespread praise, several off-field controversies dominated headlines throughout the tournament.
Political tensions surrounding Iran's participation, visa disputes, travel restrictions affecting supporters from several nations, debate over immigration policies, concerns about extreme summer heat and criticism of FIFA's growing commercialisation all fuelled discussion during the competition.
The relationship between US President Donald Trump and FIFA president Gianni Infantino also came under intense scrutiny, particularly after Balogun's suspension was lifted ahead of the United States' Round of 16 clash.
Infantino celebrates success despite criticism
Despite the criticism, FIFA has described the tournament as one of the most successful in history.
Infantino highlighted record attendance figures of nearly seven million spectators, while estimating that more than six billion viewers watched the tournament worldwide.
Television audiences also remained strong. Spanish-language broadcaster Telemundo reported that the Spain-Argentina final attracted a preliminary audience of 22.6 million, putting it on course to become the most-watched World Cup final in Spanish-language television history.
Attendance across the United States, Canada and Mexico also exceeded expectations despite concerns over ticket prices and logistics.
On the field, the expanded 48-team format largely silenced fears that the competition would become diluted. Surprise packages such as Cape Verde and Congo enjoyed memorable campaigns before the tournament ultimately delivered heavyweight semi-finals featuring Spain, Argentina, France and England.

title: 'It's embarrassing': Argentina's World Cup final conduct under fresh scrutiny
content:
FIFA World Cup final defeat to Spain has come under renewed scrutiny after new footage surfaced on social media appearing to show an attempt to influence the aftermath of Enzo Fernandez's decisive red card.
Lionel Scaloni's side lost 1-0 after extra time at New York New Jersey Stadium, with Ferran Torres scoring the winner in the 106th minute to hand Spain their second World Cup title. However, much of the post-match discussion has centred on Argentina's conduct during and after the final.
half stoppage time. Just days after netting the winner against England in the semi-finals, the Chelsea midfielder endured a frustrating evening, receiving a second yellow card in the 93rd minute following a late challenge on Spain defender Pau Cubarsi.
Fernandez immediately protested the decision, but replays showed the tackle left little doubt over the referee's call.
Fresh footage circulating online has added another layer to the controversy. The video appears to show Argentina full-back Nicolas Tagliafico standing unchallenged near Spain's penalty area before suddenly clutching his ankle and falling to the ground moments after Fernandez committed the foul.
Seconds earlier, Tagliafico had been walking normally, with no Spain player in close proximity.
The same footage also captures
Lionel Messi rushing towards referee Slavko Vincic immediately after the red card was shown. The Argentina captain pointed towards the grounded Tagliafico, seemingly attempting to draw the referee's attention to the incident. Although any stoppage for Tagliafico would have had little bearing on the outcome, Argentina appeared hopeful it might prompt officials to revisit the sequence that led to Fernandez's dismissal.
The effort had no effect, with Fernandez's red card standing and Argentina forced to see out the remainder of the match with 10 players.
Scaloni initially reshuffled his side by asking Julian Alvarez to drop deeper and help cover the numerical disadvantage. As Spain continued to dominate possession and territory, the Argentina coach eventually introduced an additional defender, leaving Messi increasingly isolated in attack.
Spain's pressure was rewarded early in extra time when substitute Ferran Torres found the net in the 106th minute, sealing a historic World Cup triumph.
Tempers flared once the final whistle blew. Nahuel Molina appeared to strike Spain captain Rodri in the torso during the celebrations, while Leandro Paredes grabbed Eric Garcia by the throat before pushing Gavi to the ground, sparking a heated confrontation involving players from both teams.
The ugly scenes drew strong criticism from former England defender Micah Richards, who questioned Argentina's behaviour after the defeat.
"It's embarrassing. They were on the cusp every single time. Seeing that sort of stuff after the game was played, it's not good, it's embarrassing to be honest. Paredes is better than that. We all know what it's like to lose a game but this is not good enough."
Wayne Rooney also criticised Argentina's reaction, saying the conduct reflected frustration after being second best on the night.
"We've seen this from Argentina before. It's just sad, very poor from them. If you lose a game of football, be graceful. It's a reaction to them being outplayed. Enzo Fernandez has got what he deserves."
